title: Correcting the Incorrect Fibonacci Sequence Generation

statement.markdown: You are given a method that aims to generate the Fibonacci sequence up to a given number n. However, the current implementation contains a logical bug and does not produce the correct sequence. Your task is to identify and correct the bug to ensure the method generates the Fibonacci sequence correctly.
statement.inputFormat: The method takes an integer n as input, where n is the number of terms in the Fibonacci sequence.
statement.outputFormat: The method should return a list of integers representing the Fibonacci sequence up to the nth term.
statement.notes: The Fibonacci sequence starts with 0 and 1, and each subsequent number is the sum of the previous two.

constraints.description: The number of terms in the Fibonacci sequence

examples.explanation: The Fibonacci sequence up to the 5th term
The Fibonacci sequence up to the 8th term

hints.text: Carefully examine the initial conditions of the Fibonacci sequence
Check the recursion or iterative approach used to generate the sequence

editorial.title: Correcting the Fibonacci Sequence Generation
editorial.intuition: The Fibonacci sequence can be generated using either a recursive or iterative approach. The key is to ensure the base cases and the recursive/iterative steps are correctly implemented.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute-force approach to generate the Fibonacci sequence involves calculating each term recursively or iteratively, but this can be inefficient for large values of n.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution involves using dynamic programming to store and reuse previously calculated Fibonacci numbers.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses a bottom-up dynamic programming approach to generate the Fibonacci sequence efficiently.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proven by showing that the generated sequence satisfies the properties of the Fibonacci sequence.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the solution involves testing it with sample inputs to ensure it produces the correct output.

starterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: std::vector<int> generateFibonacci(int n) { // TODO: implement } };
starterCode.java: class Solution { public int[] generateFibonacci(int n) { // TODO: implement } }
starterCode.python: class Solution: def generate_fibonacci(self, n: int) -> list[int]: pass

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: std::vector<int> generateFibonacci(int n) { std::vector<int> fib(n); fib[0] = 0; fib[1] = 1; for (int i = 2; i < n; i++) fib[i] = fib[i-1] + fib[i-2] + 2; return fib; } };
buggyStarterCode.java: class Solution { public int[] generateFibonacci(int n) { int[] fib = new int[n]; fib[0] = 0; fib[1] = 1; for (int i = 2; i < n; i++) fib[i] = fib[i-1] + fib[i-2] + 2; return fib; } }
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def generate_fibonacci(self, n: int) -> list[int]: fib = [0] * n; fib[0] = 0; fib[1] = 1; for i in range(2, n): fib[i] = fib[i-1] + fib[i-2] + 2; return fib;
buggyStarterCode.javascript: function generateFibonacci(n) { let fib = new Array(n); fib[0] = 0; fib[1] = 1; for (let i = 2; i < n; i++) fib[i] = fib[i-1] + fib[i-2] + 2; return fib; }
